children.value:
In the previous section we computed the difference quotient for the parabolic
marble track at

children.html: x0=5 cmx_0 = 5~\text{cm}x0​=5 cm

children.value:
with three decreasing step sizes and
obtained the values

. The numbers were converging,
but we stopped short of asking where they converge to. The difference quotient
measures average steepness over a finite interval. What we really want is the
steepness at a single point, the instantaneous slope of the rail precisely at

children.html: x0=5 cmx_0 = 5~\text{cm}x0​=5 cm

children.value:
. To find it, we must let the step size shrink all the way
to zero and examine what the difference quotient approaches in that process.
You understand the concept of the

children.html: h→0h \to 0h→0
f′(x0)=lim⁡h→0f(x0+h)−f(x0)hf'(x_0) = \lim_{h \to 0} \dfrac{f(x_0 + h) - f(x_0)}{h}f′(x0​)=h→0lim​hf(x0​+h)−f(x0​)​

children.value: as the instantaneous rate of change of

children.html: fff
x0x_0x0​

children.value:
You can interpret the differential quotient geometrically as the slope
of the

children.html: fff
(x0,f(x0))(x_0, f(x_0))(x0​,f(x0​))

children.value: What happens as the step size approaches zero?

children.label: What happens as the step size approaches zero?

children.value: At the end of section 5.1 we observed that the secant line through

children.html: P1=(5, f(5))P_1 = (5,\, f(5))P1​=(5,f(5))
P2=(5+Δx, f(5+Δx))P_2 = (5 + \Delta x,\, f(5 + \Delta x))P2​=(5+Δx,f(5+Δx))
Δx\Delta xΔx

children.value:
decreases. The table below continues that observation with even
smaller step sizes.

children.html: Δx\Delta xΔx

children.value: The values are clearly settling toward

. They never reach it for any
finite

children.html: Δx\Delta xΔx

children.value:
, but they get arbitrarily close. In mathematics we express
this by saying that the difference quotient has a

children.html: Δx\Delta xΔx
lim⁡Δx→0f(5+Δx)−f(5)Δx=−0.4.\lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{f(5 + \Delta x) - f(5)}{\Delta x} = -0.4.Δx→0lim​Δxf(5+Δx)−f(5)​=−0.4.

children.value: This limiting value is the instantaneous steepness of the rail at

children.html: x0=5 cmx_0 = 5~\text{cm}x0​=5 cm

children.value:
. It is not an average over any interval, no matter how
short. It is the slope at a single point.
The limiting process above can be applied at any point

in the domain,

children.value:
The differential quotient and the derivative at a point are two names for
the same object. The notation

children.html: f′(x0)f'(x_0)f′(x0​)

children.value:
was introduced by Lagrange and is
the most common in engineering mathematics. An alternative notation due to
Leibniz writes the same quantity as

children.html: dfdx∣x0\dfrac{df}{dx}\big|_{x_0}dxdf​∣∣​x0​​

children.value:
, which makes
the limit of the ratio

children.html: Δf/Δx\Delta f / \Delta xΔf/Δx

children.value:
visible in the symbol itself.
Both notations appear in engineering literature, and we will use them
interchangeably.
How do we compute the differential quotient for the marble track?

children.label: How do we compute the differential quotient for the marble track?

children.value: We now verify the limiting value

algebraically, using the same
technique that the German algebraist Carl Friedrich Gauss might have called
“expanding and cancelling.” This method works for any polynomial and
produces the exact limit without any guesswork.

children.html: f(x)=0.02 x2−0.6 x+6f(x) = 0.02\,x^2 - 0.6\,x + 6f(x)=0.02x2−0.6x+6
f′(5)f'(5)f′(5)

children.value:
. We start from the difference quotient and expand the numerator by
substituting

children.html: x0+Δx=5+Δxx_0 + \Delta x = 5 + \Delta xx0​+Δx=5+Δx

f(5+Δx)=0.02 (5+Δx)2−0.6 (5+Δx)+6=0.02 (25+10 Δx+(Δx)2)−3−0.6 Δx+6=0.5+0.2 Δx+0.02 (Δx)2−3−0.6 Δx+6=3.5−0.4 Δx+0.02 (Δx)2.\begin{align*} f(5 + \Delta x) &= 0.02\,(5 + \Delta x)^2 - 0.6\,(5 + \Delta x) + 6 \\ &= 0.02\,(25 + 10\,\Delta x + (\Delta x)^2) - 3 - 0.6\,\Delta x + 6 \\ &= 0.5 + 0.2\,\Delta x + 0.02\,(\Delta x)^2 - 3 - 0.6\,\Delta x + 6 \\ &= 3.5 - 0.4\,\Delta x + 0.02\,(\Delta x)^2. \end{align*}f(5+Δx)​=0.02(5+Δx)2−0.6(5+Δx)+6=0.02(25+10Δx+(Δx)2)−3−0.6Δx+6=0.5+0.2Δx+0.02(Δx)2−3−0.6Δx+6=3.5−0.4Δx+0.02(Δx)2.​
f(5)=3.5f(5) = 3.5f(5)=3.5
Δx\Delta xΔx

f(5+Δx)−f(5)Δx=3.5−0.4 Δx+0.02 (Δx)2−3.5Δx=−0.4 Δx+0.02 (Δx)2Δx=Δx (−0.4+0.02 Δx)Δx=−0.4+0.02 Δx.\begin{align*} \frac{f(5 + \Delta x) - f(5)}{\Delta x} &= \frac{3.5 - 0.4\,\Delta x + 0.02\,(\Delta x)^2 - 3.5}{\Delta x} \\ &= \frac{-0.4\,\Delta x + 0.02\,(\Delta x)^2}{\Delta x} \\ &= \frac{\Delta x\,(-0.4 + 0.02\,\Delta x)}{\Delta x} \\ &= -0.4 + 0.02\,\Delta x. \end{align*}Δxf(5+Δx)−f(5)​​=Δx3.5−0.4Δx+0.02(Δx)2−3.5​=Δx−0.4Δx+0.02(Δx)2​=ΔxΔx(−0.4+0.02Δx)​=−0.4+0.02Δx.​
Δx\Delta xΔx

children.value: out of the numerator and cancelled it with the

children.html: Δx\Delta xΔx

children.value:
in the denominator. The result is a simple linear expression with
no division by zero. Letting

children.html: Δx→0\Delta x \to 0Δx→0
f′(5)=lim⁡Δx→0(−0.4+0.02 Δx)=−0.4.f'(5) = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} (-0.4 + 0.02\,\Delta x) = -0.4.f′(5)=Δx→0lim​(−0.4+0.02Δx)=−0.4.

children.value: This confirms the numerical observation from the table. At

children.html: x0=5 cmx_0 = 5~\text{cm}x0​=5 cm

children.value: the rail is descending at an instantaneous rate of

children.html: 0.4 cm0.4~\text{cm}0.4 cm

children.value:
of height
per centimetre of horizontal travel, and the negative sign tells us it is
indeed descending rather than rising.

As a consistency check we can anticipate what the same calculation would give
at the vertex

children.html: x0=15 cmx_0 = 15~\text{cm}x0​=15 cm

children.value:
. The rail is neither rising nor falling at
its lowest point, so we expect the instantaneous slope to be zero there. The
next section derives the general formula

children.html: f′(x)=0.04 x−0.6f'(x) = 0.04\,x - 0.6f′(x)=0.04x−0.6
xxx
f′(15)=0.04⋅15−0.6=0f'(15) = 0.04 \cdot 15 - 0.6 = 0f′(15)=0.04⋅15−0.6=0

children.value: What does the differential quotient look like on the graph?

children.label: What does the differential quotient look like on the graph?

children.value: In section 5.1 we saw the secant line rotating as

children.html: Δx\Delta xΔx

children.value:
decreased.
The differential quotient is the slope that the secant approaches in the
limit. The line with exactly that slope, passing through the point

children.html: P1=(5, f(5))=(5, 3.5)P_1 = (5,\, f(5)) = (5,\, 3.5)P1​=(5,f(5))=(5,3.5)
x0=5x_0 = 5x0​=5

children.value: The tangent line touches the parabola at

children.html: P1P_1P1​

children.value:
and nowhere else in its
immediate vicinity. Its slope,

, is the best possible linear
description of how the rail behaves right at that point. If we zoomed in
on the graph around

children.html: P1P_1P1​

children.value:
, magnifying more and more, the parabola would
look increasingly straight, and that straight line would be the tangent.
This is why the derivative is sometimes described as the slope of the curve
at a point: the curve and its tangent are indistinguishable under sufficient
magnification.

The full equation of the tangent line, and its role as a linear approximation
of the function, will be developed in section 5.5. For now it is enough to
know that the differential quotient

children.html: f′(x0)f'(x_0)f′(x0​)

children.value:
SVELTE APP: "Convergence to the differential quotient"

  Two panels side by side (or stacked on narrow screens).

  LEFT PANEL: convergence table
    A live table with six rows, one for each of:
      Δx = 2, 1, 0.5, 0.1, 0.01, 0.001
    Columns: Δx | f(x0 + Δx) | Δf | difference quotient Δf/Δx
    All values computed from f(x) = 0.02x² - 0.6x + 6.
    A slider lets the student choose x0 in [0, 28], default 5.
    The bottom row of the table is highlighted and labelled
    "approaches f'(x0) = ..." with the analytical value 0.04·x0 - 0.6.

  RIGHT PANEL: graph
    The parabola f(x) = 0.02x² - 0.6x + 6 on [0, 30] in dark blue.
    The point P1 = (x0, f(x0)) as a filled dot (orange).
    A second point P2 = (x0 + Δx, f(x0 + Δx)) as a filled dot (red),
    connected to P1 by the secant line (red).
    The tangent line at x0 (green dashed), always visible.
    A small dropdown or button row lets the student pick which Δx row
    in the table is currently highlighted, so P2 jumps to the
    corresponding position and the secant updates.

  Info tile below both panels:
    "Tangent slope f'(x0)" showing 0.04·x0 - 0.6 to 3 decimal places.

  Purpose: students see the algebraic convergence in the table and the
  geometric rotation of the secant in the graph simultaneously. Moving
  x0 to 15 shows the slope approaching 0, confirming that the tangent is
  horizontal at the vertex.
Video “What is a derivative?” by Mario’s Math Tutoring
Video “MATH180 - Section 1.4: Differentiation Using Limits of Difference Quotients” by Matthew Sokol

children.title: MATH180 - Section 1.4: Differentiation Using Limits of Difference Quotients

children.value:
We have defined the differential quotient as the limit of the difference
quotient as the step size approaches zero. For the parabolic marble track at

children.html: x0=5 cmx_0 = 5~\text{cm}x0​=5 cm

children.value:
, a result we confirmed both
numerically from a convergence table and algebraically by expanding and
cancelling. Geometrically, the differential quotient is the slope of the
tangent line to the curve at the chosen point. In the computation above we
fixed

children.html: x0=5x_0 = 5x0​=5

children.value:
and obtained a single number. But the same algebraic steps
work for any

children.html: x0x_0x0​

children.value: , and the result would be a formula in

children.html: x0x_0x0​

children.value:
rather than
a single value. That formula is called the derivative function, and
constructing it is the subject of the next section.
